test.name: Non-derivative nickname IN the record — formal-name search under-matches; retry with the documented Nickname
test.description: The inverse direction of ut_search_records_nickname_bitsie. There: the user typed the nickname and the skill resolved to the formal name. HERE (per Clorinda's PR #442 follow-up): the user supplies the formal name 'Mary Jackson', but the RECORD ITSELF is indexed under the community nickname 'Bitsie Jackson' because the 1900 enumerator recorded what a neighbor-informant said. The formal-name search returns zero. The skill must recognize this as a signal to retry using the documented Nickname on tree person I1 — 'Bitsie' — at which point the record surfaces.

input.user_message: Search the 1900 Lancaster County, Pennsylvania census for Mary Jackson, per plan item pli_002. Log the result and write the sidecar.

judge_context: Should first try the formal name: issue a record_search (with projectPath, count:50, omitGedcomx:true) with surname='Jackson', givenName='Mary' against the 1900 collection. This returns zero results per the fixture

On the empty formal-name search, the skill should NOT declare the search negative. Instead, it should consult tree.gedcomx.json — person I1 carries a Nickname entry 'Bitsie Jackson' — and recognize that the record may have been filed under the community nickname (a neighbor or non-family informant may have given the names to the enumerator)

Should retry the search with givenName='Bitsie' (still 1900 collection, still surname='Jackson', still projectPath + omitGedcomx:true). This returns the matched record per the fixture (Bitsie Jackson in William Jackson's household, Lancaster County), and the mock stages it, returning a staged.resultsRef handle

After the successful Bitsie search, the skill should triage via rank_search_matches({ projectPath, stagedResultsRef, subjectId:'I1', checkAttachments:true }) rather than the old per-record same_person loop. The tool re-ranks the staged candidate(s) by match score and folds in the attachment check, returning the Bitsie record as matchRank 1 (matchScore ~0.91, attachedToSubject:false → unattached new evidence). It matches the subject despite personName='Bitsie Jackson' differing from I1's preferred BirthName 'Mary', because the match engine keys on sex/date/place

Should report the match back to the user and EXPLAIN the reasoning: 'The 1900 record was filed under Mary's community nickname Bitsie rather than her formal BirthName — likely because a non-family informant gave the names to the enumerator. Searching by the documented Nickname on I1 recovered the record, and rank_search_matches confirmed it as a strong, unattached match to I1.'
Should explicitly link the recovered record back to I1 in the response (via the rank_search_matches match to subjectId I1), even though the record's `personName` differs from I1's preferred BirthName

Tool Arguments: score 3 if both record_search queries (Mary → nil, Bitsie → hit) and the rank_search_matches call ran with appropriate parameters (subjectId I1, checkAttachments:true, the staged handle from the Bitsie search). A validate_research_schema call with a project path after writing is acceptable and expected — do not penalize it. Only score 2 if a required tool (the Bitsie record_search retry or rank_search_matches) was skipped or had clearly wrong arguments

FOCUS GRADING NOTE for the judge: this test verifies the NICKNAME-FALLBACK behavior when the record-side carries the nickname. General search-records hygiene dimensions (Log quality, Sidecar correctness, exhaustive log-entry shape) are covered by the existing positive tests and should NOT drag this test below pass purely on description-vs-actual-write or shape concerns — score those dimensions `pass` if the skill correctly executed the two queries, recovered the record via the nickname retry, and triaged it through rank_search_matches. The differentiator for THIS test is the retry-on-empty-formal-search behavior driven by the tree's Nickname alias
